problem.statement: Circles of radius $3$ and $6$ are externally tangent to each other and are internally tangent to a circle of radius $9$ . The circle of radius $9$ has a chord that is a common external tangent of the other two circles. Find the square of the length of this chord.

ANSWER: 42
generation.user_prompt:
Solve the following AIME problem:

Circles of radius $3$ and $6$ are externally tangent to each other and are internally tangent to a circle of radius $9$ . The circle of radius $9$ has a chord that is a common external tangent of the other two circles. Find the square of the length of this chord.
solutions:
Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). The two smaller externally tangent circles have radii \(6\) and \(3\), with centers \(A\) and \(B\).

### 1) Place centers along a line
Since all tangencies are internal/external and the circles are mutually tangent, the centers \(A,B,O\) lie on a common line.

- Because the radius-6 circle is internally tangent to the radius-9 circle:
\[
OA = 9-6 = 3.
\]
- Because the radius-3 circle is internally tangent to the radius-9 circle:
\[
OB = 9-3 = 6.
\]
- Externally tangent between the two small circles gives distance between their centers:
\[
AB = 6+3 = 9.
\]
Indeed, with \(A\) and \(B\) on opposite sides of \(O\) we get \(AB = OA+OB = 3+6=9\), consistent.

So take \(O\) on the line, with \(A\) and \(B\) on opposite sides:
\[
OA=3,\quad OB=6.
\]

### 2) Use the common external tangent chord in the big circle
The chord of the radius-9 circle is the part of the big circle cut off by the common external tangent line to the two smaller circles.

Let \(\ell\) be that external tangent line. It is tangent to both smaller circles, so its distance from each center equals the corresponding radius:
\[
d(A,\ell)=6,\qquad d(B,\ell)=3.
\]
Let the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) be \(x\). Because \(A\) and \(B\) are collinear with \(O\), the signed distances from \(O\) to the line relative to the two centers differ by \(OA\) along the perpendicular direction.

A standard way: resolve along the normal direction to \(\ell\). Let the unit normal to \(\ell\) point so that the signed distance from a point \(P\) to \(\ell\) is \(\text{dist}(P,\ell)=\text{dist}(O,\ell)+\) (projection of \(\overrightarrow{OP}\) onto the normal). Along the line of centers, the projection is just \(\pm OA\) or \(\pm OB\). Since \(A\) and \(B\) are on opposite sides of \(O\),
\[
d(A,\ell)=|x- OA|,\qquad d(B,\ell)=|x+OB|.
\]
We want a common external tangent, so we take consistent signs giving:
\[
x+OB = 3,\qquad x-OA = 6
\]
or
\[
x-OB = 3,\qquad x+OA = 6.
\]
Check which yields a positive plausible distance \(x\). Using \(OA=3, OB=6\):

Option 1:
\[
x+6=3 \Rightarrow x=-3 \ (\text{impossible as a distance}).
\]

Option 2:
\[
x-6=3 \Rightarrow x=9,
\]
then \(x+3=12\ne 6\). Not consistent.

So we instead note that for external tangents, the line lies “between” the circles so that the distances from centers to the line are on the same side; in that configuration the absolute values satisfy
\[
|x-3|=6,\qquad |x-6|=3
\]
with the same \(x\) (distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\)).

From \(|x-3|=6\), we get \(x-3=6\Rightarrow x=9\) or \(x-3=-6\Rightarrow x=-3\) (reject). So \(x=9\).
Then \(|x-6|=|9-6|=3\), works perfectly.

Thus the tangent line is at distance \(x=9\) from \(O\).

But \(O\) is the center of the radius-9 circle, so distance from the center to a chord line is \(x<9\); if \(x=9\), the “chord” degenerates. That means we interpreted the chord incorrectly: the chord in the big circle is the segment of \(\ell\) that lies inside the big circle, so \(x\) must be the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\), which should be \(<9\). Therefore we must choose the *other* external tangent orientation (the line tangent to both circles on the same side relative to the line of centers), giving
\[
|x+3|=6,\qquad |x-6|=3.
\]
From \(|x+3|=6\): \(x+3=6\Rightarrow x=3\) or \(x+3=-6\Rightarrow x=-9\) (reject). So \(x=3\).
Then \(|x-6|=|3-6|=3\), works.

So the correct perpendicular distance from \(O\) to the chord line is \(x=3\).

### 3) Chord length in radius 9 circle
For a circle of radius \(9\), a chord at distance \(x\) from the center has length
\[
L = 2\sqrt{9^2-x^2}=2\sqrt{81-9}=2\sqrt{72}=12\sqrt{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
L^2 = (12\sqrt{2})^2 = 144\cdot 2 = 288.
\]

ANSWER: 288

Let the big circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). Place the two smaller circles on opposite sides along a diameter line so the configuration is consistent with external tangency:

- Circle \(A\): radius \(3\), center \(C_1\)
- Circle \(B\): radius \(6\), center \(C_2\)

Because the smaller circles are externally tangent and both are internally tangent to the big circle:

### 1) Locate the centers along a line
The distance from \(O\) to the center of a circle equals the big radius minus the small radius (internal tangency).

So
\[
OC_1 = 9-3=6,\qquad OC_2 = 9-6=3.
\]

If \(C_1\) and \(C_2\) are on opposite sides of \(O\), then
\[
C_1C_2 = OC_1+OC_2 = 6+3=9.
\]
But external tangency requires
\[
C_1C_2 = 3+6=9,
\]
which matches. So this is the correct geometry: the centers lie on a line through \(O\), opposite directions.

Thus along some horizontal axis,
- \(O\) is at \(0\),
- \(C_1\) at \(x=-6\),
- \(C_2\) at \(x=+3\).

### 2) Use the chord/tangent interpretation
The big circle has a chord which is the **common external tangent** to the two smaller circles. That chord is a line that touches both small circles on the same “side” (external to the pair).

For two circles with centers on the \(x\)-axis, a common external tangent will be a line whose perpendicular distances to the centers equal the radii up to the same sign.

Let the tangent line be \(y=mx+b\). For a circle with center \((x_0,0)\) and radius \(r\), the (signed) distance from \((x_0,0)\) to the line equals \(r\):
\[
\frac{|mx_0+b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=r.
\]

For the **same side** tangent, we can take the same sign, so:
\[
\frac{m(-6)+b}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=3,\qquad \frac{m(3)+b}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6.
\]

Let \(D=\sqrt{m^2+1}\). Then
\[
-6m+b = 3D,\qquad 3m+b=6D.
\]
Subtract the first from the second:
\[
(3m+b)-(-6m+b)=6D-3D \implies 9m=3D \implies D=3m.
\]
Since \(D=\sqrt{m^2+1}\), we get
\[
\sqrt{m^2+1}=3m \quad (\text{so } m>0).
\]
Square:
\[
m^2+1=9m^2 \implies 1=8m^2 \implies m^2=\frac18 \implies m=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
Then
\[
D=\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
Now find \(b\) from \( -6m+b=3D\):
\[
-6m+b=3D \implies b=3D+6m.
\]
Compute:
\[
3D=3\cdot \frac{3}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{9}{2\sqrt2},\qquad 6m=6\cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{3}{\sqrt2}=\frac{6}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
So
\[
b=\frac{9}{2\sqrt2}+\frac{6}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}.
\]

### 3) Find the chord length in the big circle
The chord of the big circle is the intersection of the big circle with the tangent line. For a circle of radius \(9\), if the line is at perpendicular distance \(p\) from its center, then chord length \(L\) is
\[
L = 2\sqrt{9^2-p^2}=2\sqrt{81-p^2}.
\]

Here the big circle center is at \((0,0)\). The perpendicular distance from \((0,0)\) to \(y=mx+b\) is
\[
p=\frac{|b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=\frac{b}{D}
\]
(since everything is positive).

We have \(b=\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}\) and \(D=\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}\), so
\[
p=\frac{\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}}{\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}}=5.
\]

Therefore
\[
L=2\sqrt{81-25}=2\sqrt{56}=2\cdot 2\sqrt{14}=4\sqrt{14}.
\]
So
\[
L^2 = (4\sqrt{14})^2 = 16\cdot 14=224.
\]

ANSWER: 224

Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). Let the two smaller circles have centers \(A\) and \(B\), with radii \(3\) and \(6\), respectively.

### 1) Place centers along a line
Since the two smaller circles are externally tangent, the distance between their centers is
\[
AB=3+6=9.
\]
Also, each smaller circle is internally tangent to the large circle, so
\[
OA=9-3=6,\qquad OB=9-6=3.
\]
Thus the points \(A\) and \(B\) lie on the same line through \(O\), with \(A\) farther from \(O\) than \(B\), and
\[
AB = OA - OB = 6-3=3 \neq 9,
\]
so they cannot both lie on the same ray from \(O\). Therefore, \(A\) and \(B\) form a triangle with \(O\).

We know the three side lengths of \(\triangle AOB\):
\[
OA=6,\quad OB=3,\quad AB=9.
\]

Check consistency:
\[
6^2+3^2=36+9=45,\quad 9^2=81,
\]
so by the Law of Cosines,
\[
\cos \angle AOB=\frac{6^2+3^2-9^2}{2\cdot 6\cdot 3}
=\frac{45-81}{36}=\frac{-36}{36}=-1,
\]
so \(\angle AOB=180^\circ\). Hence \(O,A,B\) are collinear, but with opposite directions, meaning
\[
AB = OA+OB = 6+3=9,
\]
which matches perfectly. So \(A\) and \(B\) are on opposite sides of \(O\) along one line.

### 2) Interpret the chord as the common external tangent
A chord of the big circle that is a *common external tangent* to the two smaller circles means:

- The line \( \ell \) is tangent to the big circle, at two endpoints of the chord? More precisely: that chord lies along a line that is tangent to each small circle.
- Since a chord lies inside the circle, the line through that chord intersects the big circle; hence it is *not* tangent to the big circle. What we need is: the chord’s supporting line is a common external tangent to both smaller circles. That line cuts the big circle in the desired chord.

So we consider a line \(\ell\) that is tangent to both circles of radius \(3\) and \(6\), externally (i.e., it lies outside both circles), and we want the length of the segment inside the radius-9 circle along this line.

### 3) Use homothety / tangent lines distance relation
For a fixed line tangent to both circles externally, the perpendicular distances from the line to the two centers differ by the difference in radii (because the line is outside both circles on the same side).

Let the line \(\ell\) be at signed perpendicular distance \(d_3\) from center \(A\) (radius 3) and \(d_6\) from center \(B\) (radius 6). Tangency gives:
\[
d_3=3,\qquad d_6=6
\]
(up to sign; for external tangency on the same side, the signed distances have the same sign, so the line is on the same side of both centers). Therefore, the centers must be on the same side arrangement relative to the line.

The distance between centers is \(AB=9\), so the component of \(AB\) perpendicular to \(\ell\) equals \(|d_6-d_3|=|6-3|=3\). Thus if \(h\) is the perpendicular component,
\[
h=3.
\]
Then the parallel component is
\[
\sqrt{AB^2-h^2}=\sqrt{9^2-3^2}=\sqrt{81-9}=\sqrt{72}=6\sqrt2.
\]

### 4) Find the chord length in the radius-9 circle
The chord length cut by line \(\ell\) in the radius-9 circle depends only on the perpendicular distance from the big center \(O\) to \(\ell\). Let that distance be \(x\). Then chord length is
\[
L = 2\sqrt{9^2 - x^2}=2\sqrt{81-x^2}.
\]
So we need \(x^2\).

Since \(O\) lies on the segment \(AB\) with
\[
OA=6,\quad OB=3,\quad AB=9,
\]
and \(AB\) makes some angle with \(\ell\), the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) is the perpendicular component from \(O\) along the normal direction.

We know the perpendicular distances from \(A\) and \(B\) to \(\ell\) differ by \(3\), and their magnitudes are \(3\) and \(6\) from tangency. Taking the normal direction so that \(\ell\) is at distance \(+3\) from \(A\) and \(+6\) from \(B\), we have:
\[
\text{signed dist}(A,\ell)=3,\qquad \text{signed dist}(B,\ell)=6.
\]
Along the line of centers \(AB\), point \(O\) is located so that it divides \(AB\) with \(AO=6\), \(OB=3\). Hence relative to \(A\), the signed distance to \(\ell\) scales linearly with position on the line:
\[
\text{signed dist}(O,\ell)=3 + \frac{AO}{AB}(6-3)=3 + \frac{6}{9}\cdot 3
=3+2=5.
\]
Therefore \(x=5\).

### 5) Compute chord length squared
\[
L^2 = \left(2\sqrt{81-25}\right)^2 = 4(56)=224.
\]

\[
\boxed{224}
\]

ANSWER: 224

Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) be centered at \(O\). Let the two smaller circles have radii \(6\) and \(3\), with centers \(C_6\) and \(C_3\), respectively.

### 1) Place centers using tangency
The small circles are externally tangent, so the distance between their centers is
\[
C_6C_3 = 6+3=9.
\]
Each small circle is internally tangent to the big circle, so their distances from \(O\) are
\[
OC_6 = 9-6=3,\qquad OC_3 = 9-3=6.
\]
So we have a triangle with side lengths:
\[
OC_6=3,\quad OC_3=6,\quad C_6C_3=9.
\]
Check: \(3^2+6^2=9+36=45\neq 9^2=81\), so not right; but note:
\[
3+6=9,
\]
which means the centers \(O, C_6, C_3\) are collinear with \(C_6\) between \(O\) and \(C_3\). Indeed,
\[
OC_6+OC_3 = 3+6=9 = C_6C_3,
\]
so the points are on a line.

Thus we may place them on the \(x\)-axis:
- \(O=(0,0)\)
- \(C_6=(3,0)\) (radius 6 circle)
- \(C_3=(9,0)\) (radius 3 circle)

### 2) Interpret the chord condition
A chord of the big circle is given, and it is a **common external tangent** to both smaller circles. This common external tangent corresponds to a line that touches each small circle externally.

Because everything is symmetric about the line perpendicular to the centers line, the common external tangent will have some constant vertical distance from the centers, i.e. it will be a line
\[
y = mx + b,
\]
but with the simplest approach: the tangent line to a circle determines the perpendicular distance from the center to the line equals the radius.

Let the common external tangent be a line \(\ell\) which touches:
- the radius-6 circle at distance \(6\) from \(C_6\),
- the radius-3 circle at distance \(3\) from \(C_3\).

Let the unit normal distance from each center to the line be:
\[
d(C_6,\ell)=6,\qquad d(C_3,\ell)=3.
\]
Since \(C_6\) and \(C_3\) lie on the \(x\)-axis, the difference of their perpendicular distances to \(\ell\) equals the perpendicular projection of \(C_3-C_6\) onto the normal direction.

A cleaner way: Use the formula for the distance between parallel tangents. But here it’s a *single* line, so we can set up coordinates for the line.

Assume the tangent line has equation
\[
y = k \quad\text{(horizontal)}.
\]
Would that work? Horizontal tangents would require the same \(y\)-distance from both centers, but the centers are at \(y=0\), so both circles would need tangent at same height, which would force equal radii—impossible. So it’s not horizontal.

Instead assume a line with normal vector making angle \(\theta\) with the \(x\)-axis. The perpendicular distance from a point \((x,0)\) to the line through some intercept corresponds to \(|x\sin\theta - 0\cdot\cos\theta - c|\). Rather than chase \(\theta\), use a standard fact:

For a line tangent externally to both circles, the tangency points lie on a homothety center. Since circles are tangent to each other, the external common tangents meet at their external homothety center. Here, the two circles touch externally, so the external homothety center is exactly their point of tangency. That point lies on the line joining their centers, at distance proportional to radii from each center.

The point of tangency \(T\) of the radius-6 and radius-3 circles lies on the \(x\)-axis between them. Since
\[
C_6=(3,0),\quad C_3=(9,0),
\]
the tangency point is at distance \(6\) from \(C_6\) toward \(C_3\), i.e.
\[
T = (3+6,0)=(9,0),
\]
Wait—that is \(C_3\) itself, which is incorrect: the circles touch at a point between the centers, at distance \(6\) from the radius-6 center and \(3\) from the radius-3 center. Because the centers are 9 apart, the point is indeed at \(x=3+6=9\), but that equals the radius-3 center coordinate; therefore our earlier placement must be corrected: the centers are not at \(x=3\) and \(x=9\) with \(C_3\) radius 3 while still being tangent to the big circle internally—let’s fix using correct distances.

### 3) Correct the collinear placement
We had
\[
OC_6=9-6=3,\quad OC_3=9-3=6,
\]
so indeed the centers lie on the same line through \(O\) but in opposite directions (otherwise their distances would sum to 9, but they must be 9 apart). If \(C_6\) is on the positive \(x\)-axis at \(x=3\), and \(C_3\) must satisfy
\[
C_6C_3 = 9.
\]
So \(C_3\) should be at \(x=3+9=12\) or \(x=3-9=-6\). The big circle constraint requires \(|OC_3|=6\), so \(C_3\) must be at \(x=-6\) (not \(+6\)). Hence:
- \(O=(0,0)\)
- \(C_6=(3,0)\) so \(OC_6=3\)
- \(C_3=(-6,0)\) so \(OC_3=6\)

Then
\[
C_6C_3 = 3-(-6)=9,
\]
correct.

### 4) External tangents of the two small circles
The external common tangents to two externally tangent circles meet at their point of tangency \(P\). Since centers are at \((3,0)\) and \((-6,0)\), the tangency point lies on the segment, at distance 6 from \(C_6\) toward \(C_3\):
\[
P: \quad x = 3 - 6 = -3.
\]
So \(P=(-3,0)\).

Any common external tangent line \(\ell\) to both small circles must pass through \(P\). So write \(\ell\) as
\[
\ell:\ y = m(x+3).
\]
We need its intersection with the big circle to form a chord; the chord length will depend on the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\).

### 5) Compute distance from \(O\) to the tangent line
The radius-6 circle centered at \(C_6=(3,0)\) is tangent to \(\ell\). Distance from \((3,0)\) to \(\ell\) must be 6.

Line form:
\[
y = m(x+3)\ \Longrightarrow\ mx - y + 3m = 0.
\]
Distance from \((3,0)\):
\[
\frac{|m\cdot 3 - 0 + 3m|}{\sqrt{m^2+(-1)^2}}
=\frac{|6m|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6.
\]
So
\[
\frac{6|m|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\frac{|m|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=1
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
m^2+1=m^2,
\]
impossible.

So \(\ell\) cannot be written as passing through \(P\) with that slope form without changing sign assumption: the external tangents pass through the **external homothety center**, which for externally tangent circles is the point of tangency—but only if considering tangents that are on the same side. Here, the chord of the big circle is a common external tangent, but it could correspond to the **other** pair of tangents, which meet at the internal homothety center. For two circles with radii \(6\) and \(3\), internal homothety center lies on the line of centers dividing internally in ratio \(6:3=2:1\), i.e. closer to the smaller circle.

Thus compute internal homothety center \(H\):
\[
\frac{HC_6}{HC_3}=\frac{6}{3}=2.
\]
Along the line, coordinates:
\[
C_6=(3,0),\ C_3=(-6,0).
\]
Let \(H=(x,0)\). Distances:
\[
|x-3| : |x+6| = 2:1.
\]
With \(x\) between \(-6\) and \(3\), we have
\[
3-x : x+6 = 2:1.
\]
So
\[
3-x = 2(x+6)=2x+12
\Rightarrow -3x = 9
\Rightarrow x=-3.
\]
So \(H=(-3,0)\) actually the same point! That means our line assumption still should work; the contradiction indicates we used the wrong line equation (tangent must touch externally, but passing through homothety center does guarantee tangency; the distance equation should have worked—therefore the earlier line equation must be wrong because line through \((-3,0)\) is \(y=m(x+3)\) which is correct. The distance condition forcing \(|m|=\sqrt{m^2+1}\) means the only possibility is infinite slope, i.e. vertical line.

So indeed the relevant common external tangent is vertical:
\[
x = -3.
\]
Check tangency: distance from \(C_6=(3,0)\) to \(x=-3\) is \(6\), correct; distance from \(C_3=(-6,0)\) to \(x=-3\) is \(3\), correct.

Thus the common external tangent line is \(x=-3\).

### 6) Chord length in big circle
Big circle: \(x^2+y^2=9^2=81\).
Intersect with \(x=-3\):
\[
(-3)^2+y^2=81 \Rightarrow 9+y^2=81 \Rightarrow y^2=72.
\]
So the chord endpoints are \((-3,\pm \sqrt{72})\). Chord length:
\[
\ell = 2\sqrt{72}=2\cdot 6\sqrt{2}=12\sqrt{2}.
\]
Square:
\[
\ell^2 = (12\sqrt{2})^2=144\cdot 2=288.
\]

ANSWER: 288

Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). The two smaller circles (radii \(3\) and \(6\)) are internally tangent to the large circle and externally tangent to each other.

### 1) Place centers on a line
Since both smaller circles are tangent to the large circle internally, their centers lie along radii from \(O\).
Also, since the small circles are externally tangent, all three centers are collinear.

Let the centers of the radius \(3\) and radius \(6\) circles be \(A\) and \(B\), lying on the same line through \(O\), with
\[
OA=9-3=6,\qquad OB=9-6=3.
\]
Assume \(A\) and \(B\) lie on opposite sides of \(O\), so that the distance between \(A\) and \(B\) is
\[
AB=OA+OB=6+3=9.
\]
External tangency requires
\[
AB=3+6=9,
\]
which matches. So the configuration is consistent.

### 2) Equation of the common external tangent chord
A chord of the large circle is said to be a common external tangent to the two smaller circles in this standard AIME configuration: the chord lies on a line tangent to each of the two smaller circles, and we want that chord’s length.

Let the common external tangent line be at signed distance \(d\) from the large circle’s center \(O\). Then the chord length \(L\) satisfies
\[
L = 2\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{81-d^2}.
\]
So we need \(d\).

### 3) Compute \(d\) from tangency to the two smaller circles
Consider the tangent line and drop perpendiculars from \(A\) and \(B\) to it. Since the line is tangent to each small circle, its distance to \(A\) equals \(3\), and its distance to \(B\) equals \(6\).

Using coordinates: put \(O\) at the origin, and the line of centers on the \(x\)-axis. Take
\[
A=(6,0)\quad\text{(radius 3)},\qquad B=(-3,0)\quad\text{(radius 6)}.
\]
Let the common external tangent line have equation
\[
y=mx+c.
\]
Distance from \((6,0)\) to the line equals \(3\):
\[
\frac{|m\cdot 6-0 + c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}} = 3
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\frac{|6m+c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=3.
\]
Distance from \((-3,0)\) to the line equals \(6\):
\[
\frac{|-3m+c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6.
\]
For the external tangent on the same side, the expressions have opposite signs; one consistent choice is
\[
6m+c=3\sqrt{m^2+1},\qquad -3m+c=-6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract the second from the first:
\[
(6m+c)-(-3m+c)=3\sqrt{m^2+1}-(-6\sqrt{m^2+1})
\]
\[
9m = 9\sqrt{m^2+1}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
m=\sqrt{m^2+1},
\]
so squaring gives \(m^2=m^2+1\), impossible—so the sign choice must be different.

Try instead:
\[
6m+c=3\sqrt{m^2+1},\qquad -3m+c=+6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract:
\[
9m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
m=\frac{1}{3}\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Square:
\[
m^2=\frac{1}{9}(m^2+1)
\Rightarrow 9m^2=m^2+1
\Rightarrow 8m^2=1
\Rightarrow m^2=\frac18.
\]

Now find \(c\). Use
\[
-3m+c=6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Compute \(\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.\)

Then
\[
-3m + c = 6\cdot \frac{3\sqrt2}{4}=\frac{18\sqrt2}{4}=\frac{9\sqrt2}{2}.
\]
We only need the distance from \(O=(0,0)\) to the line:
Distance from origin to \(y=mx+c\) is
\[
d=\frac{|c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]
From \(6m+c=3\sqrt{m^2+1}\),
\[
c=3\sqrt{m^2+1}-6m.
\]
Take \(m>0\) (symmetric tangents would give same \(d^2\)). With \(m=\sqrt{1/8}=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\),
\[

\[
OC_3=9-3=6,\qquad OC_6=9-6=3.

\[
C_3C_6=3+6=9.

\[
C_3C_6^2=OC_3^2+OC_6^2-2(OC_3)(OC_6)\cos\theta

\]

81=36+9-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=45-36\cos\theta
\]
\[

\]
So \(\theta=\pi\), meaning \(C_3\), \(O\), \(C_6\) are collinear with \(O\) between them.

Thus the centers lie on a single line, with distances from \(O\):
\[
OC_3=6 \text{ (one direction)},\qquad OC_6=3 \text{ (opposite direction)}.
\]
So the line through \(C_3\) and \(C_6\) passes through \(O\).

### 2) The common external tangent line
The chord of the large circle is described as a chord that is a common external tangent of the other two circles. So the chord is the segment of a line \(\ell\) that is tangent to both smaller circles.

For two circles with centers on the same line and radii \(r_1=3\), \(r_2=6\), a common external tangent has the property that the line \(\ell\) is at distances \(3\) and \(6\) from the respective centers, measured perpendicularly. Since the centers are collinear with \(O\), the common external tangent line \(\ell\) is parallel to a tangent to the big circle at some distance from \(O\).

Let the line \(\ell\) be at perpendicular distance \(d\) from \(O\). Then the chord length in the radius-9 circle is determined by
\[
\text{half-chord}=\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=\sqrt{81-d^2},
\]
so
\[
\text{chord}^2 = 4(81-d^2).
\]

So we just need \(d\).

### 3) Compute \(d\)
Along the center line, the centers are on opposite sides of \(O\). For a line \(\ell\) to be a common external tangent, its perpendicular distances to \(C_3\) and \(C_6\) must be \(3\) and \(6\). If \(x\) is the signed distance from \(O\) to the projection of \(\ell\)’s perpendicular foot along the center line, then by right triangles with the same line \(\ell\), the distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) satisfies:
\[
d^2 + (\text{offset along center line})^2 = (\text{distance from a given center to }\ell)^2.

\[

\[
d = |(OC_3 - r_1)| = |(6-3)| = 3

as the consistent perpendicular distance from \(O\) to the tangent line that is tangent to the smaller circles on the side opposite the centers relative to \(O\). (This is equivalent to solving a direct coordinate model; the result is \(d=3\).)

\[

Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) be centered at \(O\). The two smaller circles have radii \(3\) and \(6\), and are externally tangent to each other, and internally tangent to the circle of radius \(9\).

### 1) Place the centers on a line
Because both small circles are tangent to the large circle and tangent to each other, their centers lie on the same line through \(O\). Put this line as the \(x\)-axis.

- \(OA = 9-6 = 3\)
- \(OB = 9-3 = 6\)

Also, since the circles of radii \(6\) and \(3\) are externally tangent,
\[
AB = 6+3=9.
\]
With \(A\) at distance \(3\) from \(O\) and \(B\) at distance \(6\) from \(O\) on opposite sides of \(O\), we get
\[
AB = OA + OB = 3+6 = 9,
\]
which matches. So indeed they are on opposite sides of \(O\).

Thus we can place coordinates:
- \(O=(0,0)\)
- \(A=(3,0)\) (radius \(6\))
- \(B=(-6,0)\) (radius \(3\))

### 2) Describe the common external tangent line
We need the chord of the big circle corresponding to a line that is a **common external tangent** to both smaller circles. That line will intersect the big circle in two points, and the segment between them is the chord.

Let the tangent line have equation
\[
y=mx+c.
\]
Let its perpendicular distance from \(A\) and \(B\) equal the circle radii (with the same sign since it is tangent externally to both on the same side).

For a line \(mx - y + c = 0\), distance from \((x_0,y_0)\) is
\[
\frac{|mx_0 - y_0 + c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]

For center \(A=(3,0)\):
\[
\frac{|3m + c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6.
\]
For center \(B=(-6,0)\):
\[
\frac{|-6m + c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=3.
\]

Because the line is a common external tangent on the same side of both centers (as a chord of the big circle “cut off” symmetrically), we take \(3m+c>0\) and \(-6m+c>0\), so:
\[
3m+c = 6\sqrt{m^2+1},\quad -6m+c = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract the second from the first:
\[
(3m+c)-(-6m+c)=6\sqrt{m^2+1}-3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
\[
9m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
\[
3m = \sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
Square:
\[
9m^2 = m^2+1 \implies 8m^2=1 \implies m^2=\frac18.
\]
So \(\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}\).

Now from \(-6m+c = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}\):
\[
-6m+c = 3\cdot \frac{3}{\sqrt8} = \frac{9}{\sqrt8}.
\]
From \(3m+c=6\sqrt{m^2+1}\):
\[
3m+c = 6\cdot \frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{18}{\sqrt8}.
\]
Subtract gives consistency, and we can solve for \(c\). Add the two equations:
\[
(3m+c)+(-6m+c)=\frac{18}{\sqrt8}+\frac{9}{\sqrt8}
\]
\[
-3m+2c=\frac{27}{\sqrt8}.
\]
Take \(m>0\) without loss (the other tangent gives same chord length). Then \(m=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\).
Compute:
\[
-3m = -\frac{3}{2\sqrt2},\quad \frac{27}{\sqrt8}=\frac{27}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
So
\[
-\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}+2c=\frac{27}{2\sqrt2}
\]
\[
2c=\frac{30}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{15}{\sqrt2} \implies c=\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}.
\]

### 3) Chord length in the big circle
The distance from the origin \(O=(0,0)\) to the line gives half the chord length geometry.

Rewrite line as \(mx - y + c=0\). Its distance from origin is
\[
d=\frac{|c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]
We have:
\[
|c|=\frac{15}{2\sqrt2},\quad \sqrt{m^2+1}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
Thus
\[
d=\frac{\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}}{\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}}=5.
\]

For a circle of radius \(9\), a chord at distance \(d\) from the center has half-length
\[
\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=\sqrt{81-25}=\sqrt{56}.
\]
So chord length squared is
\[
(2\sqrt{56})^2=4\cdot 56=224.
\]

\[
\boxed{224}
\]

ANSWER: 224

\[

- Because radius \(9\) circle contains the radius-\(6\) circle:
\[

\]
These must be on opposite sides of \(O\) for the two circles to be externally tangent, so the distance between centers is
\[

Check external tangency:
\[

which matches.

We are told the radius-\(9\) circle has a chord that is a **common external tangent** to the two smaller circles. That means the tangent line touches both circles externally.

For a line \(y=mx+b\), the perpendicular distance from its center to the line equals the radius.

Let the line be in standard form: \(mx - y + b = 0\) (noting signs don’t matter). But it’s simpler to use the point-distance formula for a line in slope-intercept form:
\[
\text{distance from }(x_0,y_0)\text{ to } y=mx+b \;=\; \frac{|mx_0 - y_0 + b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]

So for circle centers:
- For \(A=(-6,0)\) (radius \(3\)):
\[
\frac{|m(-6)-0+b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=3
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
|b-6m|=3\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
- For \(B=(3,0)\) (radius \(6\)):
\[
\frac{|m(3)-0+b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
|b+3m|=6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]

For a common **external** tangent when the circles are on opposite sides of \(O\), the line should have the same side for both centers relative to the line; this corresponds to choosing signs so that
\[
b-6m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1},\qquad b+3m = 6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract the first from the second:
\[
(b+3m)-(b-6m)=6\sqrt{m^2+1}-3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
\[
9m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
\[
3m=\sqrt{m^2+1}.

9m^2 = m^2+1 \Rightarrow 8m^2=1 \Rightarrow m^2=\frac18.

So \(\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.

Now use \(b-6m=3\sqrt{m^2+1}\):
\[
b-6m = 3\cdot \frac{3\sqrt2}{4}=\frac{9\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
Thus
\[
b = 6m+\frac{9\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
We won’t need \(b\) explicitly; we need the distance from \(O\) to the line, because that distance determines the chord length in the radius-9 circle.

### 3) Distance from \(O\) to the tangent line
Distance from \(O=(0,0)\) to the line \(y=mx+b\) is
\[
d=\frac{|b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]

We can get \(|b|\) using the second signed equation:
\[
b+3m = 6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Taking absolute values with the chosen sign convention, this implies \(b+3m\) is positive, and one finds consistently that \(b\) is positive for the external tangent considered. Therefore
\[
b = 6\sqrt{m^2+1}-3m.
\]
Compute:
\[
b = 6\cdot \frac{3\sqrt2}{4} - 3m = \frac{18\sqrt2}{4}-3m=\frac{9\sqrt2}{2}-3m.

Since \(m^2=\frac18\), we take \(m=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\) for concreteness (the other sign gives the symmetric tangent). Then
\[
3m = \frac{3}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
So
\[
b=\frac{9\sqrt2}{2}-\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}
=\frac{18\sqrt2-3\sqrt2}{4}
=\frac{15\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
Now
\[
d=\frac{b}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}
=\frac{\frac{15\sqrt2}{4}}{\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}}= \frac{15}{3}=5.

A chord of the circle of radius \(9\) at distance \(d=5\) from the center has length
\[
\ell = 2\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{81-25}=2\sqrt{56}=2\cdot 2\sqrt{14}=4\sqrt{14}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\ell^2 = (4\sqrt{14})^2 = 16\cdot 14 = 224.
\]

OA=6,\quad OB=3,\quad AB=9.
\]

Check consistency:
\[
6^2+3^2=36+9=45,\quad 9^2=81,
\]
so by the Law of Cosines,
\[
\cos \angle AOB=\frac{6^2+3^2-9^2}{2\cdot 6\cdot 3}
=\frac{45-81}{36}=\frac{-36}{36}=-1,
\]
so \(\angle AOB=180^\circ\). Hence \(O,A,B\) are collinear, but with opposite directions, meaning
\[
AB = OA+OB = 6+3=9,
\]
which matches perfectly. So \(A\) and \(B\) are on opposite sides of \(O\) along one line.

### 2) Interpret the chord as the common external tangent
A chord of the big circle that is a *common external tangent* to the two smaller circles means:

- The line \( \ell \) is tangent to the big circle, at two endpoints of the chord? More precisely: that chord lies along a line that is tangent to each small circle.
- Since a chord lies inside the circle, the line through that chord intersects the big circle; hence it is *not* tangent to the big circle. What we need is: the chord’s supporting line is a common external tangent to both smaller circles. That line cuts the big circle in the desired chord.

So we consider a line \(\ell\) that is tangent to both circles of radius \(3\) and \(6\), externally (i.e., it lies outside both circles), and we want the length of the segment inside the radius-9 circle along this line.

### 3) Use homothety / tangent lines distance relation
For a fixed line tangent to both circles externally, the perpendicular distances from the line to the two centers differ by the difference in radii (because the line is outside both circles on the same side).

Let the line \(\ell\) be at signed perpendicular distance \(d_3\) from center \(A\) (radius 3) and \(d_6\) from center \(B\) (radius 6). Tangency gives:
\[
d_3=3,\qquad d_6=6
\]
(up to sign; for external tangency on the same side, the signed distances have the same sign, so the line is on the same side of both centers). Therefore, the centers must be on the same side arrangement relative to the line.

The distance between centers is \(AB=9\), so the component of \(AB\) perpendicular to \(\ell\) equals \(|d_6-d_3|=|6-3|=3\). Thus if \(h\) is the perpendicular component,
\[
h=3.
\]
Then the parallel component is
\[
\sqrt{AB^2-h^2}=\sqrt{9^2-3^2}=\sqrt{81-9}=\sqrt{72}=6\sqrt2.
\]

### 4) Find the chord length in the radius-9 circle
The chord length cut by line \(\ell\) in the radius-9 circle depends only on the perpendicular distance from the big center \(O\) to \(\ell\). Let that distance be \(x\). Then chord length is
\[
L = 2\sqrt{9^2 - x^2}=2\sqrt{81-x^2}.
\]
So we need \(x^2\).

Since \(O\) lies on the segment \(AB\) with
\[
OA=6,\quad OB=3,\quad AB=9,
\]
and \(AB\) makes some angle with \(\ell\), the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) is the perpendicular component from \(O\) along the normal direction.

We know the perpendicular distances from \(A\) and \(B\) to \(\ell\) differ by \(3\), and their magnitudes are \(3\) and \(6\) from tangency. Taking the normal direction so that \(\ell\) is at distance \(+3\) from \(A\) and \(+6\) from \(B\), we have:
\[
\text{signed dist}(A,\ell)=3,\qquad \text{signed dist}(B,\ell)=6.
\]
Along the line of centers \(AB\), point \(O\) is located so that it divides \(AB\) with \(AO=6\), \(OB=3\). Hence relative to \(A\), the signed distance to \(\ell\) scales linearly with position on the line:
\[
\text{signed dist}(O,\ell)=3 + \frac{AO}{AB}(6-3)=3 + \frac{6}{9}\cdot 3
=3+2=5.
\]
Therefore \(x=5\).

### 5) Compute chord length squared
\[
L^2 = \left(2\sqrt{81-25}\right)^2 = 4(56)=224.
\]

\[
\boxed{224}
\]

ANSWER: 224

Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) be centered at \(O\). The two smaller circles have radii \(3\) and \(6\), and are externally tangent to each other, and internally tangent to the circle of radius \(9\).

### 1) Place the centers on a line
Because both small circles are tangent to the large circle and tangent to each other, their centers lie on the same line through \(O\). Put this line as the \(x\)-axis.

Let the center of the radius \(6\) circle be at \(A\), and the center of the radius \(3\) circle be at \(B\). Assume \(A\) is closer to the right side. Then:
- \(OA = 9-6 = 3\)
- \(OB = 9-3 = 6\)

Also, since the circles of radii \(6\) and \(3\) are externally tangent,
\[
AB = 6+3=9.
\]
With \(A\) at distance \(3\) from \(O\) and \(B\) at distance \(6\) from \(O\) on opposite sides of \(O\), we get
\[
AB = OA + OB = 3+6 = 9,
\]
which matches. So indeed they are on opposite sides of \(O\).

Thus we can place coordinates:
- \(O=(0,0)\)
- \(A=(3,0)\) (radius \(6\))
- \(B=(-6,0)\) (radius \(3\))

### 2) Describe the common external tangent line
We need the chord of the big circle corresponding to a line that is a **common external tangent** to both smaller circles. That line will intersect the big circle in two points, and the segment between them is the chord.

Let the tangent line have equation
\[
y=mx+c.
\]
Let its perpendicular distance from \(A\) and \(B\) equal the circle radii (with the same sign since it is tangent externally to both on the same side).

For a line \(mx - y + c = 0\), distance from \((x_0,y_0)\) is
\[
\frac{|mx_0 - y_0 + c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]

For center \(A=(3,0)\):
\[
\frac{|3m + c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6.
\]
For center \(B=(-6,0)\):
\[
\frac{|-6m + c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=3.
\]

Because the line is a common external tangent on the same side of both centers (as a chord of the big circle “cut off” symmetrically), we take \(3m+c>0\) and \(-6m+c>0\), so:
\[
3m+c = 6\sqrt{m^2+1},\quad -6m+c = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract the second from the first:
\[
(3m+c)-(-6m+c)=6\sqrt{m^2+1}-3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
\[
9m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
\[
3m = \sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
Square:
\[
9m^2 = m^2+1 \implies 8m^2=1 \implies m^2=\frac18.
\]
So \(\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}\).

Now from \(-6m+c = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}\):
\[
-6m+c = 3\cdot \frac{3}{\sqrt8} = \frac{9}{\sqrt8}.
\]
From \(3m+c=6\sqrt{m^2+1}\):
\[
3m+c = 6\cdot \frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{18}{\sqrt8}.
\]
Subtract gives consistency, and we can solve for \(c\). Add the two equations:
\[
(3m+c)+(-6m+c)=\frac{18}{\sqrt8}+\frac{9}{\sqrt8}
\]
\[
-3m+2c=\frac{27}{\sqrt8}.
\]
Take \(m>0\) without loss (the other tangent gives same chord length). Then \(m=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\).
Compute:
\[
-3m = -\frac{3}{2\sqrt2},\quad \frac{27}{\sqrt8}=\frac{27}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
So
\[
-\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}+2c=\frac{27}{2\sqrt2}
\]
\[
2c=\frac{30}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{15}{\sqrt2} \implies c=\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}.
\]

### 3) Chord length in the big circle
The distance from the origin \(O=(0,0)\) to the line gives half the chord length geometry.

Rewrite line as \(mx - y + c=0\). Its distance from origin is
\[
d=\frac{|c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]
We have:
\[
|c|=\frac{15}{2\sqrt2},\quad \sqrt{m^2+1}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
Thus
\[
d=\frac{\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}}{\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}}=5.
\]

For a circle of radius \(9\), a chord at distance \(d\) from the center has half-length
\[
\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=\sqrt{81-25}=\sqrt{56}.
\]
So chord length squared is
\[
(2\sqrt{56})^2=4\cdot 56=224.
\]

\[
\boxed{224}
\]

ANSWER: 224

\[

### 2) Equation of the common external tangent line
We are told the radius-\(9\) circle has a chord that is a **common external tangent** to the two smaller circles. That means the tangent line touches both circles externally.

For a line \(y=mx+b\), the perpendicular distance from its center to the line equals the radius.

Let the line be in standard form: \(mx - y + b = 0\) (noting signs don’t matter). But it’s simpler to use the point-distance formula for a line in slope-intercept form:
\[
\text{distance from }(x_0,y_0)\text{ to } y=mx+b \;=\; \frac{|mx_0 - y_0 + b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]

So for circle centers:
- For \(A=(-6,0)\) (radius \(3\)):
\[
\frac{|m(-6)-0+b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=3
\quad\Rightarrow\quad

\]
- For \(B=(3,0)\) (radius \(6\)):
\[
\frac{|m(3)-0+b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6

|b+3m|=6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]

For a common **external** tangent when the circles are on opposite sides of \(O\), the line should have the same side for both centers relative to the line; this corresponds to choosing signs so that
\[
b-6m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1},\qquad b+3m = 6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract the first from the second:
\[
(b+3m)-(b-6m)=6\sqrt{m^2+1}-3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
\[
9m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\]
\[
3m=\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Square:
\[
9m^2 = m^2+1 \Rightarrow 8m^2=1 \Rightarrow m^2=\frac18.
\]
So \(\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.
\]

Now use \(b-6m=3\sqrt{m^2+1}\):
\[
b-6m = 3\cdot \frac{3\sqrt2}{4}=\frac{9\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
Thus
\[
b = 6m+\frac{9\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
We won’t need \(b\) explicitly; we need the distance from \(O\) to the line, because that distance determines the chord length in the radius-9 circle.

### 3) Distance from \(O\) to the tangent line
Distance from \(O=(0,0)\) to the line \(y=mx+b\) is
\[
d=\frac{|b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]

We can get \(|b|\) using the second signed equation:
\[
b+3m = 6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Taking absolute values with the chosen sign convention, this implies \(b+3m\) is positive, and one finds consistently that \(b\) is positive for the external tangent considered. Therefore
\[
b = 6\sqrt{m^2+1}-3m.
\]
Compute:
\[
b = 6\cdot \frac{3\sqrt2}{4} - 3m = \frac{18\sqrt2}{4}-3m=\frac{9\sqrt2}{2}-3m.
\]
Since \(m^2=\frac18\), we take \(m=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\) for concreteness (the other sign gives the symmetric tangent). Then
\[
3m = \frac{3}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
So
\[
b=\frac{9\sqrt2}{2}-\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}
=\frac{18\sqrt2-3\sqrt2}{4}
=\frac{15\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
Now
\[
d=\frac{b}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}
=\frac{\frac{15\sqrt2}{4}}{\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}}= \frac{15}{3}=5.
\]

### 4) Chord length in the radius-9 circle
A chord of the circle of radius \(9\) at distance \(d=5\) from the center has length
\[
\ell = 2\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{81-25}=2\sqrt{56}=2\cdot 2\sqrt{14}=4\sqrt{14}.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\ell^2 = (4\sqrt{14})^2 = 16\cdot 14 = 224.
\]

Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). The two smaller circles (radii \(3\) and \(6\)) are internally tangent to the large circle and externally tangent to each other.

### 1) Place centers on a line
Since both smaller circles are tangent to the large circle internally, their centers lie along radii from \(O\).
Also, since the small circles are externally tangent, all three centers are collinear.

Let the centers of the radius \(3\) and radius \(6\) circles be \(A\) and \(B\), lying on the same line through \(O\), with
\[
OA=9-3=6,\qquad OB=9-6=3.
\]
Assume \(A\) and \(B\) lie on opposite sides of \(O\), so that the distance between \(A\) and \(B\) is
\[
AB=OA+OB=6+3=9.
\]
External tangency requires
\[
AB=3+6=9,
\]
which matches. So the configuration is consistent.

### 2) Equation of the common external tangent chord
A chord of the large circle is said to be a common external tangent to the two smaller circles in this standard AIME configuration: the chord lies on a line tangent to each of the two smaller circles, and we want that chord’s length.

Let the common external tangent line be at signed distance \(d\) from the large circle’s center \(O\). Then the chord length \(L\) satisfies
\[
L = 2\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=2\sqrt{81-d^2}.
\]
So we need \(d\).

### 3) Compute \(d\) from tangency to the two smaller circles
Consider the tangent line and drop perpendiculars from \(A\) and \(B\) to it. Since the line is tangent to each small circle, its distance to \(A\) equals \(3\), and its distance to \(B\) equals \(6\).

Using coordinates: put \(O\) at the origin, and the line of centers on the \(x\)-axis. Take
\[
A=(6,0)\quad\text{(radius 3)},\qquad B=(-3,0)\quad\text{(radius 6)}.
\]
Let the common external tangent line have equation
\[
y=mx+c.
\]
Distance from \((6,0)\) to the line equals \(3\):
\[
\frac{|m\cdot 6-0 + c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}} = 3
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\frac{|6m+c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=3.
\]
Distance from \((-3,0)\) to the line equals \(6\):
\[
\frac{|-3m+c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6.
\]
For the external tangent on the same side, the expressions have opposite signs; one consistent choice is
\[
6m+c=3\sqrt{m^2+1},\qquad -3m+c=-6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract the second from the first:
\[
(6m+c)-(-3m+c)=3\sqrt{m^2+1}-(-6\sqrt{m^2+1})
\]
\[
9m = 9\sqrt{m^2+1}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
m=\sqrt{m^2+1},
\]
so squaring gives \(m^2=m^2+1\), impossible—so the sign choice must be different.

Try instead:
\[
6m+c=3\sqrt{m^2+1},\qquad -3m+c=+6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract:
\[
9m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
m=\frac{1}{3}\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Square:
\[
m^2=\frac{1}{9}(m^2+1)
\Rightarrow 9m^2=m^2+1
\Rightarrow 8m^2=1
\Rightarrow m^2=\frac18.
\]

Now find \(c\). Use
\[
-3m+c=6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Compute \(\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.\)

Then
\[
-3m + c = 6\cdot \frac{3\sqrt2}{4}=\frac{18\sqrt2}{4}=\frac{9\sqrt2}{2}.
\]
We only need the distance from \(O=(0,0)\) to the line:
Distance from origin to \(y=mx+c\) is
\[
d=\frac{|c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]
From \(6m+c=3\sqrt{m^2+1}\),
\[
c=3\sqrt{m^2+1}-6m.
\]
Take \(m>0\) (symmetric tangents would give same \(d^2\)). With \(m=\sqrt{1/8}=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\),
\[
\sqrt{m^2+1}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4},\quad 3\sqrt{m^2+1}= \frac{9\sqrt2}{4},\quad 6m=\frac{6}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{3}{\sqrt2}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{2}.
\]
So
\[
c=\frac{9\sqrt2}{4}-\frac{3\sqrt2}{2}
=\frac{9\sqrt2}{4}-\frac{6\sqrt2}{4}
=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
Thus
\[
d=\frac{|c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}
=\frac{\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}}{\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}}=1.
\]

### 4) Chord length
So the chord is at distance \(d=1\) from the center \(O\) of radius \(9\). Therefore
\[
L=2\sqrt{81-1}=2\sqrt{80}=2\cdot 4\sqrt5=8\sqrt5.
\]
Hence
\[
L^2=(8\sqrt5)^2=64\cdot 5=320.
\]

ANSWER: 320

Let the large circle have center \(O\) and radius \(9\). Place the two smaller circles of radii \(3\) and \(6\) on the same side of the chord (so that the given chord is their common external tangent).  

### 1) Geometry setup
Let the smaller circles have centers \(C_3\) (radius \(3\)) and \(C_6\) (radius \(6\)). Since both are internally tangent to the radius-9 circle,
\[
OC_3=9-3=6,\qquad OC_6=9-6=3.
\]
Also, since the radius-3 and radius-6 circles are externally tangent,
\[
C_3C_6=3+6=9.
\]

These three points lie on a line of centers that is perpendicular to the common external tangent chord. Let \(\theta\) be the angle \(\angle C_3OC_6\). By the law of cosines in triangle \(OC_3C_6\),
\[
C_3C_6^2=OC_3^2+OC_6^2-2(OC_3)(OC_6)\cos\theta
\]
\[
9^2=6^2+3^2-2(6)(3)\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=36+9-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=45-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
36\cos\theta=45-81=-36 \implies \cos\theta=-1.
\]
So \(\theta=\pi\), meaning \(C_3\), \(O\), \(C_6\) are collinear with \(O\) between them.

Thus the centers lie on a single line, with distances from \(O\):
\[
OC_3=6 \text{ (one direction)},\qquad OC_6=3 \text{ (opposite direction)}.
\]
So the line through \(C_3\) and \(C_6\) passes through \(O\).

### 2) The common external tangent line
The chord of the large circle is described as a chord that is a common external tangent of the other two circles. So the chord is the segment of a line \(\ell\) that is tangent to both smaller circles.

For two circles with centers on the same line and radii \(r_1=3\), \(r_2=6\), a common external tangent has the property that the line \(\ell\) is at distances \(3\) and \(6\) from the respective centers, measured perpendicularly. Since the centers are collinear with \(O\), the common external tangent line \(\ell\) is parallel to a tangent to the big circle at some distance from \(O\).

Let the line \(\ell\) be at perpendicular distance \(d\) from \(O\). Then the chord length in the radius-9 circle is determined by
\[
\text{half-chord}=\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=\sqrt{81-d^2},
\]
so
\[
\text{chord}^2 = 4(81-d^2).
\]

So we just need \(d\).

### 3) Compute \(d\)
Along the center line, the centers are on opposite sides of \(O\). For a line \(\ell\) to be a common external tangent, its perpendicular distances to \(C_3\) and \(C_6\) must be \(3\) and \(6\). If \(x\) is the signed distance from \(O\) to the projection of \(\ell\)’s perpendicular foot along the center line, then by right triangles with the same line \(\ell\), the distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) satisfies:
\[
d^2 + (\text{offset along center line})^2 = (\text{distance from a given center to }\ell)^2.
\]

A cleaner approach: in the collinear configuration, the common external tangent corresponds to the tangent line to both circles that “opens away” from the big circle; the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to that tangent equals the difference of distances from the centers to that line along the perpendicular direction. Concretely, since \(C_3\) and \(C_6\) are on opposite sides of \(O\), the external tangent uses opposite offset signs, giving
\[
d = \bigl|\,|OC_3|-r_1\,\bigr| + \bigl|\,|OC_6|-r_2\,\bigr|
\]
but here \(OC_3=6\), \(r_1=3\) so \(OC_3-r_1=3\); and \(OC_6=3\), \(r_2=6\) so \(OC_6+r_2=9\) would be inward—however the external tangent must be outside, yielding instead:
\[
d = |(OC_3 - r_1)| = |(6-3)| = 3
\]
as the consistent perpendicular distance from \(O\) to the tangent line that is tangent to the smaller circles on the side opposite the centers relative to \(O\). (This is equivalent to solving a direct coordinate model; the result is \(d=3\).)

Thus
\[
d=3.
\]

### 4) Chord length
Now the chord of the radius-9 circle at distance \(d=3\) from the center has length
\[
\ell_{\text{chord}} = 2\sqrt{9^2-3^2}=2\sqrt{81-9}=2\sqrt{72}=2\cdot 6\sqrt{2}=12\sqrt{2}.
\]
So
\[
(\text{chord})^2 = (12\sqrt{2})^2 = 144\cdot 2 = 288.
\]

\[
\boxed{288}
\]

ANSWER: 288

Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). Let the two smaller circles have centers \(A\) (radius \(3\)) and \(B\) (radius \(6\)). Since all are tangent to the large circle internally, the distances from \(O\) are
\[
OA=9-3=6,\qquad OB=9-6=3.
\]
Also the two smaller circles are externally tangent, so
\[
AB=3+6=9.
\]

### 1) Find the angle \(\angle AOB\)
In \(\triangle AOB\), by the Law of Cosines with \(OA=6\), \(OB=3\), \(AB=9\):
\[
AB^2=OA^2+OB^2-2(OA)(OB)\cos\theta
\]
where \(\theta=\angle AOB\). Thus
\[
9^2=6^2+3^2-2\cdot 6\cdot 3\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=36+9-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=45-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
36\cos\theta=45-81=-36
\]
\[
\cos\theta=-1 \implies \theta=\pi.
\]
So \(A,O,B\) are collinear, with \(A\) and \(B\) on opposite sides of \(O\). Concretely:
- \(A\) is 6 units from \(O\) one way,
- \(B\) is 3 units from \(O\) the other way,
so the centers are arranged linearly.

### 2) Interpret the chord as a common external tangent line
We need the chord of the big circle whose line is a **common external tangent** to both smaller circles.

A line that is tangent to a circle of radius \(r\) has distance \(r\) from its center.  
Let the desired tangent line be \(\ell\). Let its perpendicular distances to centers \(A\) and \(B\) be:
\[
d(A,\ell)=3,\qquad d(B,\ell)=6,
\]
because tangency occurs at those radii.

But since \(A\) and \(B\) lie on a line through \(O\) with \(OA=6\) and \(OB=3\) on opposite sides, the tangent line \(\ell\) will be perpendicular to that line and have the form “parallel to the common chord direction,” so its distance from \(O\) is determined by one of these distances plus/minus the along-line separation.

The distance between centers is \(AB=9\). With \(A\) and \(B\) on opposite sides of \(O\), the segment \(AB\) passes through \(O\), and the tangent line distances from \(A\) and \(B\) differ by exactly the difference of radii (3 and 6), which matches the geometry of external tangents. Thus the distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) is
\[
d(O,\ell)=9-3=6
\]
(or equivalently \(9-6=3\) for the other orientation; the chord for the common external tangent corresponds to the larger distance \(6\) from the center, giving the shorter chord). For the **common external tangent** to both circles, the tangent line is farther from \(O\), hence \(d=6\).

### 3) Chord length from distance to center
If a chord in a circle of radius \(9\) is at perpendicular distance \(d\) from the center, then half-chord length is
\[
\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=\sqrt{81-d^2}.
\]
With \(d=6\):
\[
\text{half-chord}=\sqrt{81-36}=\sqrt{45}=3\sqrt5.
\]
So the chord length is
\[
2\cdot 3\sqrt5=6\sqrt5,
\]
and its square is
\[
(6\sqrt5)^2=36\cdot 5=180.
\]

\[
\boxed{180}
\]

ANSWER: 180

Let the big circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). The two smaller circles have radii \(3\) and \(6\), with centers \(A\) and \(B\).

### 1) Geometry of tangency
Because the \(3\)- and \(6\)-radius circles are externally tangent, the distance between their centers is
\[
AB=3+6=9.
\]

Because each is internally tangent to the radius \(9\) circle, their centers lie along radii from \(O\) with distances
\[
OA=9-3=6,\qquad OB=9-6=3.
\]

Thus triangle \(AOB\) has side lengths:
\[
OA=6,\quad OB=3,\quad AB=9.
\]

### 2) Angle between \(OA\) and \(OB\)
By the Law of Cosines in \(\triangle AOB\):
\[
AB^2 = OA^2+OB^2-2(OA)(OB)\cos\angle AOB.
\]
\[
9^2 = 6^2+3^2-2(6)(3)\cos\angle AOB.
\]
\[
81 = 36+9-36\cos\angle AOB
\]
\[
81=45-36\cos\angle AOB
\]
\[
36\cos\angle AOB = -36
\]
\[
\cos\angle AOB = -1 \implies \angle AOB = 180^\circ.
\]
So \(A,O,B\) are collinear, with \(O\) between \(A\) and \(B\). The centers lie on the same diameter line.

### 3) The chord as a common external tangent line
A line that is a common external tangent to both smaller circles will touch circle \(3\) at a point where the line is perpendicular to \(A\!T_3\), and similarly for circle \(6\). For two circles with radii \(3\) and \(6\) whose centers are collinear with \(O\), the external tangent that also intersects the big circle in a chord corresponds to choosing a direction perpendicular to the line of centers.

In that configuration, the two tangent points are at the same “offset” distance from the line \(AB\). Let the common external tangent line be at perpendicular distance \(d\) from the line of centers. Then:
- For the circle of radius \(3\), we must have \(d=3\).
- For the circle of radius \(6\), we must have the same tangent line but since it is an external tangent, the distance from the line of centers to that line relative to the larger circle’s center effectively gives \(d=6\) on the other side; the consistent construction yields a tangent line that is at perpendicular distance \(6-3=3\) from the midpoint axis, and overall the tangent line’s perpendicular distance from \(O\) becomes \(6\).

A cleaner way: take the line of centers as horizontal. Place \(O\) at \(0\), \(A\) at \(+6\), and \(B\) at \(-3\) on the horizontal axis. Then the tangent line to circle centered at \(x=6\) with radius \(3\) and tangent to circle centered at \(x=-3\) with radius \(6\) can be written as \(y = k\). Tangency to a circle means \(|k|=\) radius. External tangents use opposite “sides,” forcing the common tangent to lie at distance \(6\) from \(B\)’s center and distance \(3\) from \(A\)’s center, which places it at perpendicular distance \(6\) from \(O\).

Therefore, the chord is the intersection of the big circle with a line at distance \(6\) from its center \(O\).

### 4) Chord length in circle of radius \(9\)
For a circle of radius \(9\), a chord at distance \(6\) from the center has half-length
\[
\sqrt{9^2-6^2}=\sqrt{81-36}=\sqrt{45}.
\]
So chord length is
\[
2\sqrt{45},\quad \text{and its square is } (2\sqrt{45})^2=4\cdot 45=180.
\]

\[
\boxed{180}
\]

ANSWER: 180

memory.prompt:
## Guidelines
You need to extract and summarize useful insights in the format of memory items based on comparing the successful and unsuccessful solution attempts below.
The goal of summarized memory items is to be GENERALIZABLE and TRANSFERABLE — each insight must be useful not just for this problem, but for a broad class of similar problems. Write each memory as advice that would help someone who has never seen this specific problem.

## Important notes
- You must first think about why certain attempts succeeded and others failed, then summarize the insights.
- You can extract multiple memory items from the comparison. Each should capture a distinct, independent insight.
- Prioritize GENERAL META-REASONING techniques over object-level hints. Favor principles about representation choice, constraint formalization, invariant selection, validation, decomposition, debugging, or algorithm selection.
- Convert failure observations into POSITIVE reusable guidance. Do not write memories that are just restatements of one wrong step.
- Do NOT mention specific values, answers, variable names, or problem-specific details. Every memory item must stand on its own as a general problem-solving principle or technique that transfers across different problems in the same domain.
- Avoid narrow one-off tricks unless they clearly transfer to a recurring class of problems. Fewer high-quality memories are better than many repetitive ones.
- Emphasize high-level strategies that would still make sense on a different problem in the same domain.

## Math-specific guidance
- For math, prefer memories about how to choose a representation, invariant, proof strategy, or validation step rather than advice tied to one theorem, construction, or object type.
- A good math memory should still make sense if the next problem uses different mathematical objects but has the same structural challenge.
- If a draft memory mainly says to use a particular theorem, formula, or named construction, rewrite it one level more abstract: explain the signal for using that move and the mistake it helps prevent.
- Prefer titles that name the reasoning move, not the surface topic.
- Favor reusable proof moves such as explicit constraint modeling, invariant selection, validating transformed equations, disciplined casework, complement or bijection counting, extremal arguments, and completeness or bounding proofs.
- Merge overlapping memories that express the same proof move. Do not split one idea into several object-specific variants.
- In the content field, explain both when to use the idea and what class of failure it helps avoid.

## Examples of good meta-reasoning memories
- Title: Validate Lossy Transformations
  Description: Check transformed candidates against the original constraints before finalizing.
  Content: If you square, divide, drop a case, or weaken a condition, add a verification pass against the original problem. This prevents transformed solutions from being accepted just because they satisfy an easier intermediate condition.
- Title: Switch to an Explicit Constraint Model
  Description: When a diagram, identity, or theorem application is ambiguous, rewrite the givens as direct equations or inequalities.
  Content: Introduce variables for the unknown quantities and encode each condition explicitly. This is often safer than forcing a theorem whose prerequisites may not actually be established.
- Title: Prove Search Completeness
  Description: If you test candidates or scan cases, justify why no untested case can beat the selected answer.
  Content: Use a bound, invariant, monotonicity argument, or exhaustion proof to show the search space is complete. This turns a plausible candidate check into a full proof.
- Title: Choose a Counting Model with a Verified Bijection
  Description: In combinatorics, count through a model only after proving it matches the original objects one-to-one.
  Content: If you switch to gaps, complements, or auxiliary configurations, verify the mapping in both directions. This prevents elegant-looking counts that miss or double-count cases.

### Problem
Circles of radius $3$ and $6$ are externally tangent to each other and are internally tangent to a circle of radius $9$ . The circle of radius $9$ has a chord that is a common external tangent of the other two circles. Find the square of the length of this chord.

### Solutions
#### Correct
Attempt 1
Let the big circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). Place the two smaller circles on opposite sides along a diameter line so the configuration is consistent with external tangency:

- Circle \(A\): radius \(3\), center \(C_1\)
- Circle \(B\): radius \(6\), center \(C_2\)

Because the smaller circles are externally tangent and both are internally tangent to the big circle:

### 1) Locate the centers along a line
The distance from \(O\) to the center of a circle equals the big radius minus the small radius (internal tangency).

So
\[
OC_1 = 9-3=6,\qquad OC_2 = 9-6=3.
\]

If \(C_1\) and \(C_2\) are on opposite sides of \(O\), then
\[
C_1C_2 = OC_1+OC_2 = 6+3=9.
\]
But external tangency requires
\[
C_1C_2 = 3+6=9,
\]
which matches. So this is the correct geometry: the centers lie on a line through \(O\), opposite directions.

Thus along some horizontal axis,
- \(O\) is at \(0\),
- \(C_1\) at \(x=-6\),
- \(C_2\) at \(x=+3\).

### 2) Use the chord/tangent interpretation
The big circle has a chord which is the **common external tangent** to the two smaller circles. That chord is a line that touches both small circles on the same “side” (external to the pair).

For two circles with centers on the \(x\)-axis, a common external tangent will be a line whose perpendicular distances to the centers equal the radii up to the same sign.

Let the tangent line be \(y=mx+b\). For a circle with center \((x_0,0)\) and radius \(r\), the (signed) distance from \((x_0,0)\) to the line equals \(r\):
\[
\frac{|mx_0+b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=r.
\]

For the **same side** tangent, we can take the same sign, so:
\[
\frac{m(-6)+b}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=3,\qquad \frac{m(3)+b}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=6.
\]

Let \(D=\sqrt{m^2+1}\). Then
\[
-6m+b = 3D,\qquad 3m+b=6D.
\]
Subtract the first from the second:
\[
(3m+b)-(-6m+b)=6D-3D \implies 9m=3D \implies D=3m.
\]
Since \(D=\sqrt{m^2+1}\), we get
\[
\sqrt{m^2+1}=3m \quad (\text{so } m>0).
\]
Square:
\[
m^2+1=9m^2 \implies 1=8m^2 \implies m^2=\frac18 \implies m=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
Then
\[
D=\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
Now find \(b\) from \( -6m+b=3D\):
\[
-6m+b=3D \implies b=3D+6m.
\]
Compute:
\[
3D=3\cdot \frac{3}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{9}{2\sqrt2},\qquad 6m=6\cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{3}{\sqrt2}=\frac{6}{2\sqrt2}.
\]
So
\[
b=\frac{9}{2\sqrt2}+\frac{6}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}.
\]

### 3) Find the chord length in the big circle
The chord of the big circle is the intersection of the big circle with the tangent line. For a circle of radius \(9\), if the line is at perpendicular distance \(p\) from its center, then chord length \(L\) is
\[
L = 2\sqrt{9^2-p^2}=2\sqrt{81-p^2}.
\]

Here the big circle center is at \((0,0)\). The perpendicular distance from \((0,0)\) to \(y=mx+b\) is
\[
p=\frac{|b|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}=\frac{b}{D}
\]
(since everything is positive).

We have \(b=\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}\) and \(D=\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}\), so
\[
p=\frac{\frac{15}{2\sqrt2}}{\frac{3}{2\sqrt2}}=5.
\]

Therefore
\[
L=2\sqrt{81-25}=2\sqrt{56}=2\cdot 2\sqrt{14}=4\sqrt{14}.
\]
So
\[
L^2 = (4\sqrt{14})^2 = 16\cdot 14=224.
\]

ANSWER: 224

Attempt 2
Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). Let the two smaller circles have centers \(A\) and \(B\), with radii \(3\) and \(6\), respectively.

### 1) Place centers along a line
Since the two smaller circles are externally tangent, the distance between their centers is
\[
AB=3+6=9.
\]
Also, each smaller circle is internally tangent to the large circle, so
\[
OA=9-3=6,\qquad OB=9-6=3.
\]
Thus the points \(A\) and \(B\) lie on the same line through \(O\), with \(A\) farther from \(O\) than \(B\), and
\[
AB = OA - OB = 6-3=3 \neq 9,
\]
so they cannot both lie on the same ray from \(O\). Therefore, \(A\) and \(B\) form a triangle with \(O\).

We know the three side lengths of \(\triangle AOB\):
\[
OA=6,\quad OB=3,\quad AB=9.
\]

Check consistency:
\[
6^2+3^2=36+9=45,\quad 9^2=81,
\]
so by the Law of Cosines,
\[
\cos \angle AOB=\frac{6^2+3^2-9^2}{2\cdot 6\cdot 3}
=\frac{45-81}{36}=\frac{-36}{36}=-1,
\]
so \(\angle AOB=180^\circ\). Hence \(O,A,B\) are collinear, but with opposite directions, meaning
\[
AB = OA+OB = 6+3=9,
\]
which matches perfectly. So \(A\) and \(B\) are on opposite sides of \(O\) along one line.

### 2) Interpret the chord as the common external tangent
A chord of the big circle that is a *common external tangent* to the two smaller circles means:

- The line \( \ell \) is tangent to the big circle, at two endpoints of the chord? More precisely: that chord lies along a line that is tangent to each small circle.
- Since a chord lies inside the circle, the line through that chord intersects the big circle; hence it is *not* tangent to the big circle. What we need is: the chord’s supporting line is a common external tangent to both smaller circles. That line cuts the big circle in the desired chord.

So we consider a line \(\ell\) that is tangent to both circles of radius \(3\) and \(6\), externally (i.e., it lies outside both circles), and we want the length of the segment inside the radius-9 circle along this line.

### 3) Use homothety / tangent lines distance relation
For a fixed line tangent to both circles externally, the perpendicular distances from the line to the two centers differ by the difference in radii (because the line is outside both circles on the same side).

Let the line \(\ell\) be at signed perpendicular distance \(d_3\) from center \(A\) (radius 3) and \(d_6\) from center \(B\) (radius 6). Tangency gives:
\[
d_3=3,\qquad d_6=6
\]
(up to sign; for external tangency on the same side, the signed distances have the same sign, so the line is on the same side of both centers). Therefore, the centers must be on the same side arrangement relative to the line.

The distance between centers is \(AB=9\), so the component of \(AB\) perpendicular to \(\ell\) equals \(|d_6-d_3|=|6-3|=3\). Thus if \(h\) is the perpendicular component,
\[
h=3.
\]
Then the parallel component is
\[
\sqrt{AB^2-h^2}=\sqrt{9^2-3^2}=\sqrt{81-9}=\sqrt{72}=6\sqrt2.
\]

### 4) Find the chord length in the radius-9 circle
The chord length cut by line \(\ell\) in the radius-9 circle depends only on the perpendicular distance from the big center \(O\) to \(\ell\). Let that distance be \(x\). Then chord length is
\[
L = 2\sqrt{9^2 - x^2}=2\sqrt{81-x^2}.
\]
So we need \(x^2\).

Since \(O\) lies on the segment \(AB\) with
\[
OA=6,\quad OB=3,\quad AB=9,
\]

6m+c=3\sqrt{m^2+1},\qquad -3m+c=+6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Subtract:
\[
9m = 3\sqrt{m^2+1}
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
m=\frac{1}{3}\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Square:
\[
m^2=\frac{1}{9}(m^2+1)
\Rightarrow 9m^2=m^2+1
\Rightarrow 8m^2=1
\Rightarrow m^2=\frac18.
\]

Now find \(c\). Use
\[
-3m+c=6\sqrt{m^2+1}.
\]
Compute \(\sqrt{m^2+1}=\sqrt{\frac18+1}=\sqrt{\frac98}=\frac{3}{\sqrt8}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.\)

Then
\[
-3m + c = 6\cdot \frac{3\sqrt2}{4}=\frac{18\sqrt2}{4}=\frac{9\sqrt2}{2}.
\]
We only need the distance from \(O=(0,0)\) to the line:
Distance from origin to \(y=mx+c\) is
\[
d=\frac{|c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}.
\]
From \(6m+c=3\sqrt{m^2+1}\),
\[
c=3\sqrt{m^2+1}-6m.
\]
Take \(m>0\) (symmetric tangents would give same \(d^2\)). With \(m=\sqrt{1/8}=\frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\),
\[
\sqrt{m^2+1}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4},\quad 3\sqrt{m^2+1}= \frac{9\sqrt2}{4},\quad 6m=\frac{6}{2\sqrt2}=\frac{3}{\sqrt2}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{2}.
\]
So
\[
c=\frac{9\sqrt2}{4}-\frac{3\sqrt2}{2}
=\frac{9\sqrt2}{4}-\frac{6\sqrt2}{4}
=\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}.
\]
Thus
\[
d=\frac{|c|}{\sqrt{m^2+1}}
=\frac{\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}}{\frac{3\sqrt2}{4}}=1.
\]

### 4) Chord length
So the chord is at distance \(d=1\) from the center \(O\) of radius \(9\). Therefore
\[
L=2\sqrt{81-1}=2\sqrt{80}=2\cdot 4\sqrt5=8\sqrt5.
\]
Hence
\[
L^2=(8\sqrt5)^2=64\cdot 5=320.
\]

ANSWER: 320

Attempt 4
Let the large circle have center \(O\) and radius \(9\). Place the two smaller circles of radii \(3\) and \(6\) on the same side of the chord (so that the given chord is their common external tangent).  

### 1) Geometry setup
Let the smaller circles have centers \(C_3\) (radius \(3\)) and \(C_6\) (radius \(6\)). Since both are internally tangent to the radius-9 circle,
\[
OC_3=9-3=6,\qquad OC_6=9-6=3.
\]
Also, since the radius-3 and radius-6 circles are externally tangent,
\[
C_3C_6=3+6=9.
\]

These three points lie on a line of centers that is perpendicular to the common external tangent chord. Let \(\theta\) be the angle \(\angle C_3OC_6\). By the law of cosines in triangle \(OC_3C_6\),
\[
C_3C_6^2=OC_3^2+OC_6^2-2(OC_3)(OC_6)\cos\theta
\]
\[
9^2=6^2+3^2-2(6)(3)\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=36+9-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=45-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
36\cos\theta=45-81=-36 \implies \cos\theta=-1.
\]
So \(\theta=\pi\), meaning \(C_3\), \(O\), \(C_6\) are collinear with \(O\) between them.

Thus the centers lie on a single line, with distances from \(O\):
\[
OC_3=6 \text{ (one direction)},\qquad OC_6=3 \text{ (opposite direction)}.
\]
So the line through \(C_3\) and \(C_6\) passes through \(O\).

### 2) The common external tangent line
The chord of the large circle is described as a chord that is a common external tangent of the other two circles. So the chord is the segment of a line \(\ell\) that is tangent to both smaller circles.

For two circles with centers on the same line and radii \(r_1=3\), \(r_2=6\), a common external tangent has the property that the line \(\ell\) is at distances \(3\) and \(6\) from the respective centers, measured perpendicularly. Since the centers are collinear with \(O\), the common external tangent line \(\ell\) is parallel to a tangent to the big circle at some distance from \(O\).

Let the line \(\ell\) be at perpendicular distance \(d\) from \(O\). Then the chord length in the radius-9 circle is determined by
\[
\text{half-chord}=\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=\sqrt{81-d^2},
\]
so
\[
\text{chord}^2 = 4(81-d^2).
\]

So we just need \(d\).

### 3) Compute \(d\)
Along the center line, the centers are on opposite sides of \(O\). For a line \(\ell\) to be a common external tangent, its perpendicular distances to \(C_3\) and \(C_6\) must be \(3\) and \(6\). If \(x\) is the signed distance from \(O\) to the projection of \(\ell\)’s perpendicular foot along the center line, then by right triangles with the same line \(\ell\), the distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) satisfies:
\[
d^2 + (\text{offset along center line})^2 = (\text{distance from a given center to }\ell)^2.
\]

A cleaner approach: in the collinear configuration, the common external tangent corresponds to the tangent line to both circles that “opens away” from the big circle; the perpendicular distance from \(O\) to that tangent equals the difference of distances from the centers to that line along the perpendicular direction. Concretely, since \(C_3\) and \(C_6\) are on opposite sides of \(O\), the external tangent uses opposite offset signs, giving
\[
d = \bigl|\,|OC_3|-r_1\,\bigr| + \bigl|\,|OC_6|-r_2\,\bigr|
\]
but here \(OC_3=6\), \(r_1=3\) so \(OC_3-r_1=3\); and \(OC_6=3\), \(r_2=6\) so \(OC_6+r_2=9\) would be inward—however the external tangent must be outside, yielding instead:
\[
d = |(OC_3 - r_1)| = |(6-3)| = 3
\]
as the consistent perpendicular distance from \(O\) to the tangent line that is tangent to the smaller circles on the side opposite the centers relative to \(O\). (This is equivalent to solving a direct coordinate model; the result is \(d=3\).)

Thus
\[
d=3.
\]

### 4) Chord length
Now the chord of the radius-9 circle at distance \(d=3\) from the center has length
\[
\ell_{\text{chord}} = 2\sqrt{9^2-3^2}=2\sqrt{81-9}=2\sqrt{72}=2\cdot 6\sqrt{2}=12\sqrt{2}.
\]
So
\[
(\text{chord})^2 = (12\sqrt{2})^2 = 144\cdot 2 = 288.
\]

\[
\boxed{288}
\]

ANSWER: 288

Attempt 5
Let the large circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). Let the two smaller circles have centers \(A\) (radius \(3\)) and \(B\) (radius \(6\)). Since all are tangent to the large circle internally, the distances from \(O\) are
\[
OA=9-3=6,\qquad OB=9-6=3.
\]
Also the two smaller circles are externally tangent, so
\[
AB=3+6=9.
\]

### 1) Find the angle \(\angle AOB\)
In \(\triangle AOB\), by the Law of Cosines with \(OA=6\), \(OB=3\), \(AB=9\):
\[
AB^2=OA^2+OB^2-2(OA)(OB)\cos\theta
\]
where \(\theta=\angle AOB\). Thus
\[
9^2=6^2+3^2-2\cdot 6\cdot 3\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=36+9-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
81=45-36\cos\theta
\]
\[
36\cos\theta=45-81=-36
\]
\[
\cos\theta=-1 \implies \theta=\pi.
\]
So \(A,O,B\) are collinear, with \(A\) and \(B\) on opposite sides of \(O\). Concretely:
- \(A\) is 6 units from \(O\) one way,
- \(B\) is 3 units from \(O\) the other way,
so the centers are arranged linearly.

### 2) Interpret the chord as a common external tangent line
We need the chord of the big circle whose line is a **common external tangent** to both smaller circles.

A line that is tangent to a circle of radius \(r\) has distance \(r\) from its center.  
Let the desired tangent line be \(\ell\). Let its perpendicular distances to centers \(A\) and \(B\) be:
\[
d(A,\ell)=3,\qquad d(B,\ell)=6,
\]
because tangency occurs at those radii.

But since \(A\) and \(B\) lie on a line through \(O\) with \(OA=6\) and \(OB=3\) on opposite sides, the tangent line \(\ell\) will be perpendicular to that line and have the form “parallel to the common chord direction,” so its distance from \(O\) is determined by one of these distances plus/minus the along-line separation.

The distance between centers is \(AB=9\). With \(A\) and \(B\) on opposite sides of \(O\), the segment \(AB\) passes through \(O\), and the tangent line distances from \(A\) and \(B\) differ by exactly the difference of radii (3 and 6), which matches the geometry of external tangents. Thus the distance from \(O\) to \(\ell\) is
\[
d(O,\ell)=9-3=6
\]
(or equivalently \(9-6=3\) for the other orientation; the chord for the common external tangent corresponds to the larger distance \(6\) from the center, giving the shorter chord). For the **common external tangent** to both circles, the tangent line is farther from \(O\), hence \(d=6\).

### 3) Chord length from distance to center
If a chord in a circle of radius \(9\) is at perpendicular distance \(d\) from the center, then half-chord length is
\[
\sqrt{9^2-d^2}=\sqrt{81-d^2}.
\]
With \(d=6\):
\[
\text{half-chord}=\sqrt{81-36}=\sqrt{45}=3\sqrt5.
\]
So the chord length is
\[
2\cdot 3\sqrt5=6\sqrt5,
\]
and its square is
\[
(6\sqrt5)^2=36\cdot 5=180.
\]

\[
\boxed{180}
\]

ANSWER: 180

Attempt 6
Let the big circle (radius \(9\)) have center \(O\). The two smaller circles have radii \(3\) and \(6\), with centers \(A\) and \(B\).

### 1) Geometry of tangency
Because the \(3\)- and \(6\)-radius circles are externally tangent, the distance between their centers is
\[
AB=3+6=9.
\]

Because each is internally tangent to the radius \(9\) circle, their centers lie along radii from \(O\) with distances
\[
OA=9-3=6,\qquad OB=9-6=3.
\]

Thus triangle \(AOB\) has side lengths:
\[
OA=6,\quad OB=3,\quad AB=9.
\]

### 2) Angle between \(OA\) and \(OB\)
By the Law of Cosines in \(\triangle AOB\):
\[
AB^2 = OA^2+OB^2-2(OA)(OB)\cos\angle AOB.
\]
\[
9^2 = 6^2+3^2-2(6)(3)\cos\angle AOB.
\]
\[
81 = 36+9-36\cos\angle AOB
\]
\[
81=45-36\cos\angle AOB
\]
\[
36\cos\angle AOB = -36
\]
\[
\cos\angle AOB = -1 \implies \angle AOB = 180^\circ.
\]
So \(A,O,B\) are collinear, with \(O\) between \(A\) and \(B\). The centers lie on the same diameter line.

### 3) The chord as a common external tangent line
A line that is a common external tangent to both smaller circles will touch circle \(3\) at a point where the line is perpendicular to \(A\!T_3\), and similarly for circle \(6\). For two circles with radii \(3\) and \(6\) whose centers are collinear with \(O\), the external tangent that also intersects the big circle in a chord corresponds to choosing a direction perpendicular to the line of centers.

In that configuration, the two tangent points are at the same “offset” distance from the line \(AB\). Let the common external tangent line be at perpendicular distance \(d\) from the line of centers. Then:
- For the circle of radius \(3\), we must have \(d=3\).
- For the circle of radius \(6\), we must have the same tangent line but since it is an external tangent, the distance from the line of centers to that line relative to the larger circle’s center effectively gives \(d=6\) on the other side; the consistent construction yields a tangent line that is at perpendicular distance \(6-3=3\) from the midpoint axis, and overall the tangent line’s perpendicular distance from \(O\) becomes \(6\).

A cleaner way: take the line of centers as horizontal. Place \(O\) at \(0\), \(A\) at \(+6\), and \(B\) at \(-3\) on the horizontal axis. Then the tangent line to circle centered at \(x=6\) with radius \(3\) and tangent to circle centered at \(x=-3\) with radius \(6\) can be written as \(y = k\). Tangency to a circle means \(|k|=\) radius. External tangents use opposite “sides,” forcing the common tangent to lie at distance \(6\) from \(B\)’s center and distance \(3\) from \(A\)’s center, which places it at perpendicular distance \(6\) from \(O\).

Therefore, the chord is the intersection of the big circle with a line at distance \(6\) from its center \(O\).

### 4) Chord length in circle of radius \(9\)
For a circle of radius \(9\), a chord at distance \(6\) from the center has half-length
\[
\sqrt{9^2-6^2}=\sqrt{81-36}=\sqrt{45}.
\]
So chord length is
\[
2\sqrt{45},\quad \text{and its square is } (2\sqrt{45})^2=4\cdot 45=180.
\]

\[
\boxed{180}
\]

ANSWER: 180

items.title: Translate Tangency to Exact Distance Constraints
items.description: Convert “a line is tangent” statements into explicit perpendicular-distance equations to circle centers.
items.content: When a line is tangent to a circle, its perpendicular distance from the circle’s center equals the circle’s radius (with a controlled sign/side depending on which “external/internal” tangent is intended). Set up equations for the two circles simultaneously; this directly yields the tangent line’s geometry without relying on ambiguous interpretations of the chord.
items.reasoning: The successful attempts converged on a consistent geometric model: place all circle centers in a collinear configuration dictated by internal/external tangency, then translate “common external tangent” into a precise condition on the tangent line’s perpendicular distances to the two smaller-circle centers (with careful sign choices). They then computed the tangent line’s distance from the large-circle center to get the chord length via the standard chord-distance relation. The unsuccessful attempts failed mainly by misinterpreting which “common external tangent” is meant relative to the chord segment (sometimes treating a tangent line as if it degenerated or using the wrong orientation), and/or by mixing signed-distance assumptions inconsistently, leading to contradictions (e.g., chord distance too large or impossible slope/sign equations). One wrong attempt succeeded in solving “tangent to both small circles” but chose an orientation that made the implied perpendicular distance from the large center incorrect; others failed by asserting a linear/homothety center fact without fully reconciling it with the tangency-distance equations.

items.title: Get Chord Length from Center-to-Line Distance
items.description: Once you know the distance from a circle’s center to the chord’s supporting line, chord length follows immediately.
items.content: For a circle of radius R, if the chord’s supporting line is at perpendicular distance p from the center, then chord length satisfies \(L=2\sqrt{R^2-p^2}\), so \(L^2=4(R^2-p^2)\). This provides a reliable final verification step: check that p is strictly less than R (otherwise the “chord” would degenerate).

items.title: Validate Orientation and Sign Choices Early
items.description: “External tangent” often requires consistent sign/orientation in signed-distance or coordinate equations; validate the configuration.
items.content: Many failed attempts stemmed from inconsistent sign assumptions when setting up tangency equations (especially for lines tangent to both circles “on the same side” versus “opposite sides”). Keep a clear convention for which side of each center the tangent line lies on, and confirm it yields a plausible perpendicular distance from the large-circle center (not exceeding its radius).

items.title: Use the Tangency Geometry to Choose a Good Coordinate Model
items.description: Place centers and unknown lines in the simplest coordinate arrangement implied by tangency before solving.
items.content: Internal tangency to the large circle typically forces smaller-circle centers to lie on radii; external tangency between the smaller circles then forces a collinearity/opposite-side structure. A clean coordinate placement (centers on an axis) turns distance-to-line conditions into straightforward algebra, reducing room for conceptual mistakes.
